Cast NiMnGa samples, of Ni 50 Mn 30 Ga 20 nominal composition, were prepared by 5 arc melting cycles of the pure elements (electrolytic Ni 99.97%, electrolytic Mn 99.5% and Ga 99.99%) in stoichiometric ratio, in a non-consumable electrode furnace (Leybold LK6 / 45) (Leybold, Cologne, Germany). As-cast ingot was ground to powder in a planetary ball mill (Fritsch Pulverisette 4) (Fritsch, Idar-Oberstein, Germany) and the powder size selected by means of sieves. Densiﬁed pellets were produced by die pressing alloy powders with di ﬀ erent average size (lower than 50 µ m or between 50 and 100 µ m) at 0.75 GPa at room temperature and sintered by a thermal treatment at 925 ◦ C for 24, 72, and 168 h in an Ar atmosphere, followed by slow cooling in the furnace. To prevent the non-isothermal precipitation and grain growth, the extrudates were quenched in water at the exit of the extrusion. Typically, an alloy system that displays a persistent liquid phase will show shrinkage after sintering. In the case of a transient liquid phase the components of the liquid are ab- sorbed into the matrix grains increasing their volume. As more of the liquid phase is absorbed, the dimensions of the sample will increase. As can be seen in Figure 4, this is exactly the case in the P/M AZ31 alloy. The dimensional change data depicted in Figure 4 is independent of the other sintering variables of compaction, time and quench temper- ature. The result is a swelling of more than 3 % in the max- imum dimension at 600 °C. At the lowest temperature, the liquid phase persists long enough to allow sufﬁcient den- siﬁcation before being absorbed to counteract the porosity after it is absorbed, and results in slightly over 1 % swelling. Results of the Vickers hardness test on Ti-V-O coatings after HTA are shown in Figure 4 . # 2. Experiments 
# 2.1. Specimen Materials 
The material used here, both for specimens and fretting pads, was TC11 (Ti-6.5Al-1.5Zr-3.5Mo-0.3Si) titanium alloy. TC11 titanium alloy is widely used in the China aviation ﬁeld, and the chemical composition of this alloy (in wt.%) is given in Table 1 [ 30 ]. # Materials: GBE-type processing and bicrystal preparation 
The material chosen for the intergranular-degradation studies was alloy 718, a polycrystalline Ni-based superal- loy that makes up of approximately 40% of all Ni-based alloys used in aero engines. GBE-type processing led to an increase in the fraction of special CSL grain boundaries ( R 3– R 29) from a minimum value of f CSL = 0.149 in the as-received condition to a maximum value of f CSL = 0.42 in the 4-cycle GBE TMP condition, i.e. a thickness reduction by 20% followed by a 1h anneal at 1,050  C was applied four times. It should be mentioned that approximately 80% of the CSL grain boundaries were R 3 and 4% were R 9.

# 4.5. Hardness testing 
From the detailed microstructure analysis performed, it is not dif ﬁ cult to attribute the material properties to the precipitation process. The effects of different solution heat treatment times on Inconel 718 (IN718) formed by high-deposition-rate laser metal deposition (HDR-LMD) have been studied in this current work. # 2. Experimental procedures 
The chemical composition of investigated CoNi-base superalloys, CoNi-1, CoNi-2, and CoNi-5, were measured by the X-ray ﬂ uorescence (XRF) technique and the results are given in Table 1 . Single crystalline specimens were produced by using a Bridgeman furnace with a with- drawal rate of 6 mm/min. The specimens were grown along the [001] crystal orientation and the deviation was determined to be less than 10° utilizing the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Investigated al- loys were homogenized at 1250 °C for 24 h, subsequently aged at 900 °C for 168 h. 
The CALPHAD method [24,25] was used to calculate the γ ′ volume fraction and the γ ′ solvus temperature of investigated alloys utilizing software Thermo-Calc TCNI8 database (version 8.1) [26] which con- tains the newest thermodynamic data of Co-base and Ni-base super- alloys.

In order to investigate the microstructure evolution of the alloy, the solid solution treatment and short-term aging (1523 K/16 h/AC + 1123 K/24 h/FC) are carried out on the HT4 alloy. The typical microstructure of the HT4 alloy is shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (b). Obviously, the precipitates along the NiAl grain bound- ary become ﬁne and distribute un-continuously, but along the NiAl grain boundary there is still a thin ﬁlm of precipitate.
